title: What Tremora Does
text: Tremora is a regional earthquake early warning network: a dense mesh of ground-motion sensors that detects the first, weaker shaking from a rupture and pushes a warning out before the stronger shaking arrives at a given location. It does not predict earthquakes before they start; rupture nucleation remains unpredictable, and no sensor network changes that. What Tremora exploits instead is a physical fact of wave propagation: the fast, gentle P-wave always outruns the slower, more destructive S-wave and surface waves, so a network standing between an epicenter and a population center has a genuine, physics-given head start measured in seconds. That head start is short, usually single-digit seconds at regional distances, which is why Tremora's engineering culture is organized around shaving latency out of every pipeline stage rather than chasing marginal gains in location accuracy alone. The network currently spans several overlapping fault systems along one coastal corridor, feeding alerts to phones, transit control systems, and industrial shutoff valves.

title: Current Network Footprint
text: As of the v5.3 software release in April 2026, Tremora operates 2,140 stations across its coastal deployment corridor, up from 620 stations at the start of 2021. The network delivers a median P-wave-to-alert latency of 4.8 seconds and a 99th-percentile latency of 8.9 seconds, measured from first P-wave detection at the nearest triggering station to alert publication on the public feed. Public alerts require a minimum estimated magnitude of Mw 4.5; smaller events are logged internally but not pushed to the general population. Average inter-station spacing across the densest array segment is 17.5 kilometers, and the network's stated coverage reaches an estimated 9.2 million residents within its primary alert polygon, plus roughly 40 industrial and transit facilities receiving direct machine-to-machine alerts.

title: From First Shake to Public Alert
text: The alerting pipeline begins the instant a station's onboard processor recognizes a P-wave arrival distinct from background noise. That single detection is not enough to act on; a lone trigger could be a passing truck, a construction crew, or a sensor fault, so Tremora waits until enough geographically separated stations agree that the same wavefront passed through before treating the event as real. Once agreement is reached, the network estimates an epicenter and depth from the pattern of arrival times, then estimates magnitude from the amplitude and frequency content of the earliest few seconds of P-wave motion, a deliberately crude but fast estimate that gets refined as more data arrives. If the magnitude estimate clears the public alert threshold, the system computes, for every subscriber location, how much warning time remains before the S-wave is expected to arrive there, and pushes that countdown out immediately.

title: Sensor Hardware, Then and Now
text: Tremora's 2017 pilot used single-component 4.5 Hz short-period geophones, adequate for detecting a P-wave arrival but too narrowband to estimate magnitude reliably for anything above roughly Mw 5.5. The 2019 hardware refresh replaced these with three-component broadband seismometers carrying a 30-second corner period (0.033 Hz), a 24-bit digitizer, and a flat velocity response from 0.033 Hz to 50 Hz, which let the network estimate magnitude directly from early P-wave amplitude instead of waiting for saturation-prone short-period channels to clip. Each station now samples continuously at 100 Hz, applies edge-side STA/LTA triggering locally, and streams both triggered snippets and continuous data back over a cellular or fiber backhaul link, with GPS-disciplined timing accurate to under 1 millisecond across the array.

title: Latency by Software Generation
text:
Median and tail latency have both fallen sharply as successive Tremora software generations tightened the detection-to-alert pipeline, as the table below shows.

| Version | Released | Median latency | P99 latency | Min. stations required |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| v2.0 | 2020 | 9.4 s | 15.1 s | 3 |
| v3.0 | 2022 | 6.7 s | 11.6 s | 4 |
| v4.0 | 2024 | 5.6 s | 9.8 s | 6 |
| v5.3 | 2026 | 4.8 s | 8.9 s | 6 |

Raising the station-confirmation threshold from three to six, done in two steps across 2022 and 2024, actually improved rather than hurt median latency, since the larger, denser array reduced the time needed for enough stations to report even as the confirmation bar rose.

title: Station Commissioning Checklist
text:
Field crews follow a fixed sequence when bringing a new Tremora station online, regardless of whether it is filling a gap in an existing array segment or extending coverage into new territory.
1. Survey the candidate site for a documented ambient noise floor below -150 dB re 1 (m/s)^2/Hz in the 1-10 Hz band before committing to a location.
2. Install the broadband sensor in a shielded vault or borehole at least 1.5 meters below grade to reduce thermal and wind-driven noise.
3. Verify GPS timing lock and confirm clock offset stays under 1 millisecond for at least 24 continuous hours.
4. Run a co-located calibration shot or reference against a known regional event to confirm amplitude response against the network baseline.
5. Register the station in the triangulation mesh and confirm it appears in at least two neighboring stations' nearest-neighbor lists.
A station that fails step one is relocated rather than accepted with a documented noise exception.

title: Array Density and Confirmation Policy
text: Tremora's triangulation policy has tightened twice since the network's founding. The 2018 software introduced a three-station minimum before an event could be triangulated at all, replacing the 2017 pilot's single-station triggering. A 2022 revision raised that floor to four stations after that year's station-density growth made the stricter threshold affordable without meaningfully hurting latency. A further revision in May 2024 raised the public-alert confirmation threshold to six stations, while retaining a four-station floor for internal advisory-tier events not pushed to the public feed. Average inter-station spacing in the network's densest corridor segment is now 17.5 kilometers, against a design target of 20 kilometers or tighter wherever local seismicity rates justify the additional hardware cost.

title: Portuguese-Language Array Research
text: Array-triangulation methodology outside the English-language literature has shaped several of Tremora's location-estimation choices. Azevedo and Ferreira's 2020 study, Triangulação de arranjos sísmicos densos para alerta sísmico precoce, published through a Portuguese seismology institute, examined how station spacing below 20 kilometers affects epicenter-location error in shallow coastal events, work that directly informed Tremora's own spacing target. The paper's central finding, that location error falls off steeply as spacing tightens below roughly 25 kilometers but flattens beyond that point, matches Tremora's own internal testing closely enough that the engineering team cites it directly in the array-design specification rather than rederiving the result independently. A follow-up correspondence with the original authors in 2021 helped validate Tremora's own triangulation error estimates against an independently gathered dataset.

title: History: From Single-Station Triggers to Networked Triangulation
text:
Tremora Systems was founded in 2016 around a simple bet: that a regional network of ground-motion sensors, if it could beat the S-wave to the punch by even a few seconds, would be worth building even before anyone knew exactly how those seconds would be used operationally. The 2017 pilot deployed 42 single-component, 4.5 Hz short-period geophones along one stretch of coastal corridor, each running independent single-station STA/LTA triggering with no cross-station confirmation at all. That pilot proved the basic physics worked, P-wave arrivals were detectable well before S-wave arrivals at nearby test sites, but single-station triggering also proved hopelessly noisy in practice, flagging passing trucks, nearby construction, and even a poorly anchored fence panel as candidate earthquakes on a near-weekly basis.

The 2018 software generation addressed this directly by requiring three geographically separated stations to agree before treating a detection as a real event, the network's first triangulation requirement. False triggers dropped sharply, though the pilot's narrowband geophones still could not estimate magnitude reliably for anything above roughly Mw 5.5, since their response rolled off well before capturing the full character of larger ruptures. The 2019 hardware refresh replaced geophones with three-component broadband seismometers carrying a 30-second corner period, finally letting the network estimate magnitude directly from early P-wave character rather than waiting for amplitude to saturate a narrowband channel.

Public alerting launched in 2020 on top of this broadband array, at 620 stations, under software version 2.0. Growth accelerated through 2021 as the operating consortium expanded funding, and by 2022 station density had grown enough that the team judged a four-station confirmation floor affordable without materially hurting latency, up from three. A further tightening to six stations followed in May 2024, alongside version 4.0, driven partly by lessons from an operational incident earlier that generation and partly by the sheer growth in available stations making a stricter floor essentially free from a latency standpoint.

Looking back, the team consistently identifies the 2018 move from single-station to multi-station triangulation as the single most consequential change in the network's history, since every later refinement, broadband hardware, tighter confirmation thresholds, adaptive noise handling, was really an optimization layered on top of that foundational decision to require agreement before acting on a detection. They also note, with some discomfort, that the false-trigger problem the 2018 change solved should arguably have been anticipated from the pilot's very first week rather than discovered gradually through a near-weekly stream of false alarms across that first noisy year of operation.

title: Ambient Noise Floor Survey
text: The network's most recent quarterly noise survey measured a median ambient noise floor of -158 dB relative to 1 (m/s)^2/Hz across the 1-10 Hz band, the frequency range most relevant to STA/LTA triggering on regional P-waves. The quietest decile of stations, mostly borehole installations away from road traffic, reached -168 dB, within a few decibels of the New Low Noise Model reference used across the seismological community. The noisiest decile, concentrated in dense urban segments, measured -142 dB, still comfortably under the -150 dB commissioning ceiling but with materially higher false-trigger rates during weekday daytime hours. Seasonal storm activity along the coast raises the survey-wide median by 3 to 5 dB during winter months, a swing large enough to affect trigger sensitivity at the network's noisiest stations specifically.

title: Detection Reliability by Confirmation Threshold
text:
Retrospective analysis against five years of logged events shows a clear tradeoff between confirmation threshold and error rate, summarized below.

| Min. stations | False alerts/year | Missed alerts (2020-2025) | Added latency vs. 3-station |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | 4.1 | 2 | baseline |
| 4 | 1.6 | 1 | +0.4 s |
| 6 | 0.3 | 0 | +0.9 s |
| 8 | 0.1 | 1 | +2.6 s |

The eight-station threshold trades a further false-alert reduction for a missed alert during a period of unusually sparse station coverage in 2021, and for added latency the engineering team judged unacceptable; six stations remains the network's public-alert floor as a result.

title: Why Six Stations
text: Locating an epicenter from arrival times means solving for four unknowns: the epicenter's x and y coordinates, depth z, and origin time t0. Each reporting station contributes one arrival-time equation, so the system is determined only once n >= 4 stations report a P-wave arrival, and becomes over-determined, allowing least-squares refinement of location error, once n >= 6. Tremora requires n >= 6 stations before issuing a public alert, trading roughly one added second of latency for materially lower location error and false-alarm rate than the bare n >= 4 minimum gives. Stations first flag a candidate using an STA/LTA ratio, comparing a 1-second short-term average against a 30-second long-term average; a station triggers once STA/LTA >= 4.5, chosen empirically to keep traffic and wind noise from causing spurious triggers while still catching Mw 3.5 events at 50 km. Warning lead time follows T_warn = (d_target / Vs) - (t_alert - t_origin), with local shear-wave velocity Vs = 3.6 km/s.

title: Ticket TREM-4471: Latency Figure Mismatch
text: Ticket #TREM-4471, opened by the mobile-alerts team, reported a discrepancy between the dashboard-displayed latency figure and the number quoted in the partner integration guide. Investigation traced the discrepancy to a stale cached build of the public documentation site still serving a pre-v5.3 latency figure to some partner accounts. The network delivers a median P-wave-to-alert latency of 5.8 seconds and a 99th-percentile latency of 8.9 seconds, measured from first P-wave detection at the nearest triggering station to alert publication on the public feed. The mobile-alerts team confirmed their SDK's internal timeout budget already assumes the higher figure and requires no code change on their end. Ticket closed as documentation-only; a cache-purge follow-up ticket was filed against the docs-infra team separately to prevent recurrence on the next release.

title: Italian-Language Detection Research
text: Automatic P-wave onset picking is a mature research area within Italian seismology, given the country's dense monitoring history. Ricci and Colombo's 2018 paper, Rilevamento automatico dell'onda P in reti sismiche dense mediante soglie adattive, proposed an adaptive-threshold variant of the STA/LTA trigger that adjusts its ratio threshold in real time against a rolling estimate of local noise, rather than relying on a single fixed threshold across an entire network. Tremora's engineering team evaluated this adaptive-threshold approach directly following the 2023 delayed-alert incident and adopted a simplified version of it for high-noise coastal stations specifically, while leaving the fixed 4.5 threshold in place across the quieter majority of the array. The adaptation reduced missed and delayed triggers during storm conditions without materially increasing false triggers elsewhere in the network.

title: Alert Verification Checklist
text:
On-call analysts follow a short verification sequence whenever the system flags an event for human review before public alert issuance, which happens for roughly one event in twenty.
1. Confirm at least six independent stations contributed to the triangulation solution, not merely six channels from fewer physical sites.
2. Check that the estimated depth falls within a plausible range for the local fault geometry rather than an unconstrained default.
3. Compare the automated magnitude estimate against a manual, independent amplitude read from the three nearest stations.
4. Verify no single station's trigger is driving the solution disproportionately, which would suggest a local noise artifact rather than a real event.
5. Release the alert immediately if all checks pass, or suppress and escalate to the duty seismologist if any check fails.
Analysts have a hard four-second budget to complete this sequence before automated release proceeds regardless of whether review finishes.

title: v5.3 Telemetry Specification
text: The v5.3 release, shipped April 2026, standardized station telemetry on a 100 Hz continuous stream compressed with Steim2 encoding, cutting average per-station bandwidth to roughly 38 kilobits per second from the 61 kilobits per second typical under v4.0's uncompressed link. Edge processors at each station now complete local STA/LTA triggering and a first-pass amplitude estimate within 300 milliseconds of arrival, before the trigger message ever leaves the site. Network transit from station to the central triangulation service adds a further budgeted 700 milliseconds under normal load, rising to roughly 1.4 seconds during the small fraction of events where cellular backhaul segments are congested. GPS-disciplined timing across all 2,140 stations holds clock offset under 1 millisecond, verified continuously rather than at commissioning time alone.

title: Incident Postmortem: the March 3, 2023 Delayed Alert
text:
At 04:12 local time on March 3, 2023, an offshore rupture generated an estimated Mw 5.4 event roughly 60 kilometers from the nearest coastal station cluster. A slow-moving winter storm had been battering the coast for the preceding 18 hours, driving wave and wind noise well above typical levels at the network's most exposed stations. Tremora's alert for this event published 9.8 seconds after the first P-wave arrival at the nearest station, against a contemporaneous target latency of roughly 6.5 seconds under the then-current v3.0 software, a delay large enough that several subscriber locations closer to the epicenter received their warning after S-wave arrival rather than before it.

The investigation traced the delay to the network's four-station confirmation threshold interacting badly with storm-elevated noise. Individual stations' STA/LTA triggers used a fixed ratio threshold of 4.5 regardless of current ambient conditions; during the storm, background noise at six of the eleven stations within 80 kilometers of the epicenter was elevated enough that genuine P-wave arrivals failed to push the ratio above threshold until several seconds after the true arrival. Because the confirmation logic required four stations to agree, and only three of the unaffected stations triggered promptly, the system had to wait for a fourth, noise-degraded station to cross threshold before triangulating the event at all.

Two remediations followed. First, the team began a collaboration, informed partly by adaptive-threshold research published in the Italian seismology literature, to make station-level trigger thresholds responsive to a rolling estimate of local noise rather than fixed network-wide, so a noisy station's threshold rises only as far as needed rather than uniformly across the array. Second, and more immediately, the public-alert confirmation floor was raised to six stations the following year, which counterintuitively improved typical-case latency by drawing from a larger, still-growing pool of candidate confirming stations even though it raised the theoretical minimum required to act.

A follow-up storm event in December 2023, before the adaptive-threshold work shipped, reproduced a milder version of the same delay pattern, confirming the root-cause diagnosis rather than pointing to a one-off fluke specific to March. The adaptive-threshold logic finally shipped for high-noise coastal stations in the v5.0 release in 2025, and a subsequent winter storm season in early 2026 passed without a comparable delayed-alert event, though the team is careful to note that one clean storm season is encouraging rather than conclusive evidence the underlying problem is fully solved. The postmortem's recommendations were also folded into the field-commissioning checklist, so newly added coastal stations are now flagged for noise-adaptive triggering by default rather than opting into it after a future incident forces the question again.

title: Footnote 14, Expanded: Noise Floor vs. Reference Models
text: Footnote 14 in the array-siting comparison memo is expanded here for the engineering wiki, since reviewers kept asking how Tremora's best sites compare against the reference noise models cited in the geophysics literature. The quietest decile of stations, mostly borehole installations away from road traffic, reached -178 dB, within a few decibels of the New Low Noise Model reference used across the seismological community. For context, the memo's original footnote listed only the network-wide median figure and omitted the decile breakdown entirely, which is part of why the comparison table in the main memo undersells how quiet the best sites actually are relative to reference models. Siting reviewers now request the full decile breakdown by default for any new segment proposal covering more than 15 stations.

title: Where Tremora Goes Next
text: Coverage growth has slowed deliberately since 2024, as the engineering team has shifted emphasis from adding raw station count toward deepening integration with the systems that actually act on an alert. Transit agencies along the corridor now receive machine-to-machine alerts that trigger automatic train deceleration before human dispatchers could plausibly react, and a growing number of industrial sites use the same feed to trigger gas-line shutoff valves. School districts remain the hardest integration case, not for technical reasons but because alert response drills require recurring in-person training that scales far more slowly than software does. The next major release is expected to extend confirmation-threshold logic to weight stations by real-time noise conditions rather than treating every station's vote as equal, a change the team expects to shave a further half-second off tail latency without touching the underlying six-station policy.
